URL: https://www.cell.com/cell/fulltext/S0092-8674(19)31121-3

SUMMARY:
Definition and Characteristics of Cellular Senescence. Cellular senescence is a cell state triggered by stressful insults and certain physiological processes, characterized by a prolonged and generally irreversible cell-cycle arrest with secretory features, macromolecular damage, and altered metabolism (Figure 1).

## Contents

# Cellular senescence

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/72/Hayflick_Limit_%281%29.svg/250px-Hayflick_Limit_%281%29.svg.png)
![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7d/SABG_MEFs.jpg/250px-SABG_MEFs.jpg)

**Cellular senescence** is a phenomenon characterized by the cessation of [cell division](/wiki/Cell_division "Cell division").[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3) In their experiments during the early 1960s, [Leonard Hayflick](/wiki/Leonard_Hayflick "Leonard Hayflick") and Paul Moorhead found that normal human fetal [fibroblasts](/wiki/Fibroblast "Fibroblast") in culture reach a maximum of approximately 50 cell population doublings before becoming senescent.[[4]](#cite_note-Collado_2007-4)[[5]](#cite_note-:1-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6) This process called the [Hayflick limit](/wiki/Hayflick_limit "Hayflick limit") is also known as "replicative senescence", since it is brought about through [replication](/wiki/DNA_replication#Replication_process "DNA replication"). Hayflick's discovery of mortal cells paved the path for the discovery and understanding of cellular aging molecular pathways.[[7]](#cite_note-Shay20002-7) Cellular senescence can be initiated by a wide variety of stress-inducing factors. These stress factors include both environmental and internal damaging events, abnormal cellular growth, [oxidative stress](/wiki/Oxidative_stress "Oxidative stress"), [autophagy](/wiki/Autophagy "Autophagy") factors, among many other things.[[8]](#cite_note-Kuilman_2010-8)

The physiological importance of cell senescence has been attributed to prevention of [carcinogenesis](/wiki/Carcinogenesis "Carcinogenesis"), and more recently, aging, development, and tissue repair.[[9]](#cite_note-9) Senescent cells contribute to the aging [phenotype](/wiki/Phenotype "Phenotype"), including [frailty syndrome](/wiki/Frailty_syndrome "Frailty syndrome"), [sarcopenia](/wiki/Sarcopenia "Sarcopenia"), and [aging-associated diseases](/wiki/Aging-associated_diseases "Aging-associated diseases").[[10]](#cite_note-pmid32752135-10) Senescent [astrocytes](/wiki/Astrocyte "Astrocyte") and [microglia](/wiki/Microglia "Microglia") contribute to [neurodegeneration](/wiki/Neurodegeneration "Neurodegeneration").[[11]](#cite_note-pmid31619990-11)[[12]](#cite_note-:2-12)

## Cellular mechanisms

### Stress response and DNA damage

Mechanistically, replicative senescence can be triggered by a [DNA damage](/wiki/DNA_damage "DNA damage") response due to the shortening of [telomeres](/wiki/Telomere "Telomere"). Cells can also be induced to senesce by DNA damage in response to elevated [reactive oxygen species](/wiki/Reactive_oxygen_species "Reactive oxygen species") (ROS), activation of [oncogenes](/wiki/Oncogene "Oncogene"), and cell-[cell fusion](/wiki/Cell_fusion "Cell fusion"). Normally, cell senescence is reached through a combination of a variety of factors (i.e., both telomere shortening and oxidative stress).[[13]](#cite_note-pmid2664649922-13) The [DNA damage](/wiki/DNA_damage_(naturally_occurring) "DNA damage (naturally occurring)") response (DDR) arrests cell cycle progression until DNA damage, such as [double-strand breaks](/wiki/DNA_repair#Double-strand_breaks "DNA repair") (DSBs), is repaired. Senescent cells display persistent DDR that appears to be resistant to endogenous [DNA repair](/wiki/DNA_repair "DNA repair") activities. The prolonged DDR activates both ATM and ATR DNA damage kinases. The phosphorylation cascade initiated by these two kinases causes the eventual arrest of the cell cycle. Depending on the severity of the DNA damage, the cells may no longer be able to undergo repair and either go through [apoptosis](/wiki/Apoptosis "Apoptosis") or cell senescence.[[8]](#cite_note-Kuilman_2010-8) Such senescent cells in mammalian culture and tissues retain DSBs and DDR markers.[[14]](#cite_note-pmid281245092-14) It has been proposed that retained DSBs are major drivers of the [aging](/wiki/Ageing "Ageing") process. Mutations in genes relating to genome maintenance have been linked with [premature aging diseases](/wiki/Progeroid_syndromes "Progeroid syndromes"), supporting the role of cell senescence in aging.[[15]](#cite_note-pmid275886012-15)

Depletion of [NAD+](/wiki/Nicotinamide_adenine_dinucleotide "Nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide") can lead to DNA damage and cellular senescence in [vascular smooth muscle](/wiki/Vascular_smooth_muscle "Vascular smooth muscle") cells.[[16]](#cite_note-pmid32298812-16)

Although senescent cells can no longer replicate, they remain metabolically active and commonly adopt an [immunogenic](/wiki/Immunogenicity "Immunogenicity") [phenotype](/wiki/Phenotype "Phenotype") that enables them to be eliminated by the immune system.[[17]](#cite_note-Cellular_senescence:_from_growth_ar-17) The phenotype consists of a [pro-inflammatory secretome](/wiki/Senescence-associated_secretory_phenotype "Senescence-associated secretory phenotype"), the up-regulation of immune ligands, a pro-survival response, promiscuous gene expression (pGE), and stain positive for [senescence-associated β-galactosidase](/wiki/Senescence-associated_beta-galactosidase "Senescence-associated beta-galactosidase") activity.[[18]](#cite_note-pmid23140366-18) Two proteins, senescence-associated beta-galactosidase and [p16Ink4A](/wiki/P16 "P16"), are regarded as [biomarkers](/wiki/Biomarker "Biomarker") of [cellular senescence](/wiki/Senescence#Cellular_senescence "Senescence"). However, this results in a false positive for cells that naturally have these two proteins, such as maturing tissue [macrophages](/wiki/Macrophage "Macrophage") with senescence-associated beta-galactosidase and [T-cells](/wiki/T-cell "T-cell") with p16Ink4A.[[13]](#cite_note-pmid2664649922-13)

The nucleus of senescent cells is characterized by senescence-associated [heterochromatin](/wiki/Heterochromatin "Heterochromatin") foci (SAHF) and [DNA segments with chromatin alterations reinforcing senescence](/wiki/DNA-SCARS "DNA-SCARS") (DNA-SCARS).[[19]](#cite_note-19) Senescent cells affect tumour suppression, wound healing, and possibly embryonic/placental development, and a pathological role in age-related diseases.[[20]](#cite_note-Physiological_and_pathological_cons-20)

### Cell growth and size

Cell growth plays a crucial role in cell proliferation, regulating cellular [homeostasis](/wiki/Homeostasis "Homeostasis") and cell cycle progression through dynamic changes in cell size.[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1) And like DNA damage, it can promote senescence by triggering a prolonged cell cycle arrest.[[5]](#cite_note-:1-5) While a typical increase in cell size controls for concentrations of cell cycle activators,[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1)[[12]](#cite_note-:2-12) an excess of growth can drive a permanent halt on cell proliferation as a result of various mechanistic interactions with cell-cycle signaling pathways and thresholds present.[[21]](#cite_note-:3-21)

As the cell increases in size without sufficient proliferation, cellular homeostasis becomes more and more difficult to achieve; the cell experiences cytoplasmic dilution and succumbs to a permanent cell cycle arrest.[[21]](#cite_note-:3-21)[[22]](#cite_note-:4-22) More particularly, the osmotic stress caused by this overgrowth is linked to an accumulation of [p21](/wiki/P21 "P21") during G0/G1 arrest, consequently preventing re-entry into [S phase](/wiki/S_phase "S phase").[[23]](#cite_note-:5-23)[[24]](#cite_note-:6-24) Additionally, the persistent growth of the cell during this arrest, as driven by [mTOR](/wiki/MTOR "MTOR") signaling, causes phenotypes characteristic of senescent cells such as cellular hypertrophy, [SASP](/wiki/Senescence-associated_secretory_phenotype "Senescence-associated secretory phenotype") and lysosomal hyperfunctions.[[25]](#cite_note-25) The enlarged cells that *are* able to re-enter the cell cycle are prone to DNA damage and experience abnormalities in signaling for repair (NHEJ pathway), eventually leading to a replication failure and a permanent cell-cycle exit.[[24]](#cite_note-:6-24)

Overall, a consistent correlation between larger cell size and senescence has been established. Understanding this mechanistic relationship is useful for addressing different treatment sensitivities in clinical contexts. For tumors presenting growth signal mutations, cell cycle inhibitors ([CDK4/6 and CDK7 inhibitors](/wiki/CDK_inhibitor "CDK inhibitor")) hold potential to be a more useful therapeutic, given this cell-size dependency of cellular senescence.[[26]](#cite_note-:7-26)[[21]](#cite_note-:3-21)

### Role of telomeres

[Telomeres](/wiki/Telomere "Telomere") are DNA tandem repeats at the end of chromosomes that shorten during each cycle of cell division.[[27]](#cite_note-27) Recently, the role of telomeres in cellular senescence has aroused general interest, especially with a view to the possible genetically adverse effects of [cloning](/wiki/Cloning "Cloning"). The successive shortening of the [chromosomal](/wiki/Chromosome "Chromosome") telomeres with each [cell cycle](/wiki/Cell_cycle "Cell cycle") is also believed to limit the number of divisions of the cell, contributing to aging. After sufficient shortening, proteins responsible for maintaining telomere structure, such as TRF2, are displaced, resulting in the telomere being recognized as a site of a double-strand break.[[21]](#cite_note-:3-21) This induces replicative senescence.[[23]](#cite_note-:5-23) Theoretically, it is possible, upon the discovery of the exact mechanism of biological immortality, to genetically engineer cells with the same capability. The length of the telomere strand has senescent effects; telomere shortening activates extensive alterations in alternative RNA splicing that produce senescent toxins such as [progerin](/wiki/Progerin "Progerin"), which degrades tissue and makes it more prone to failure.[[28]](#cite_note-pmid216704982-28)

### Role of oncogenes

BRAFV600E and Ras are two [oncogenes](/wiki/Oncogene "Oncogene") implicated in cellular senescence. BRAFV600E induces senescence through synthesis and secretion of [IGFBP7](/wiki/IGFBP7 "IGFBP7").[[24]](#cite_note-:6-24) Ras activates the [MAPK cascade](/wiki/MAPK_cascade "MAPK cascade") which results in increased p53 activation and p16INK4a upregulation.[[29]](#cite_note-Yun_2018-29) The transition to a state of senescence due to oncogene mutations are irreversible and have been termed oncogene-induced senescence (OIS).[[30]](#cite_note-Childs_2014-30)

Interestingly, even after oncogenic activation of a tissue, several researchers have identified a senescent phenotype. Researchers have[*[when?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*] identified a senescent phenotype in benign lesions of the skin carrying oncogenic mutations in neurofibroma patients with a defect that specifically causes an increase in Ras. This finding has been highly reproducible in benign prostate lesions, in melanocytic lesions of UV-irradiated HGF/SF-transgenic mice,[[31]](#cite_note-31) in lymphocytes and in the mammary gland from N-Ras transgenic mice,[[26]](#cite_note-:7-26) and in hyperplasias of the pituitary gland of mice with deregulated E2F activity.[[32]](#cite_note-pmid15767672-32) The key to these findings is that genetic manipulations that abrogated the senescence response led to full-blown malignancy in those carcinomas. As such, the evidence suggests senescent cells can be associated with pre-malignant stages of the tumor. Further, it has been speculated that a senescent phenotype might serve as a promising marker for staging. There are two types of senescence *in vitro*. The irreversible senescence, which is mediated by INK4a/Rb and p53 pathways, and the reversible senescent phenotype, which is mediated by p53. This suggests that the p53 pathway could be effectively harnessed as a therapeutic intervention to trigger senescence and ultimately mitigate tumorigenesis.[[4]](#cite_note-Collado_2007-4)

p53 has been shown to have promising therapeutic relevance in an oncological context. In the 2007 *Nature* paper by Xue et al., RNAi was used to regulate endogenous p53 in a liver carcinoma model. Xue et al. utilized a chimaeric liver cancer mouse model and transduced this model with the ras oncogene. They took embryonic progenitor cells, transduced those cells with oncogenic ras, along with the tetracycline transactivator (tta) protein to control p53 expression using doxycycline, a tetracycline analog, and tetracycline-responsive short hairpin RNA (shRNA). In the absence of Dox, p53 was actively suppressed as the microRNA levels increased, so as Dox was administered, p53 microRNA was turned off to facilitate the expression of p53. The liver cancers that expressed Ras showed signs of senescence following p53 reactivation, including an increase in senescence-associated β-galactosidase protein. Even if the expression of p53 was transiently activated or deactivated, senescence via SA β-gal was observed. Xue et al. show that by briefly reactivating p53 in tumors without functional p53 activity, tumor regression is observed. The induction of cellular senescence was associated with an increase in inflammatory cytokines, as is expected based on the SASP. The presence of both senescence and an increase in immune activity can regress and limit liver carcinoma growth in this mouse model.[[33]](#cite_note-33)

## Signaling pathways

There are several reported signaling pathways that lead to cellular senescence, including the p53 and p16Ink4a pathways.[[30]](#cite_note-Childs_2014-30) Both of these pathways are activated in response to cellular stressors and lead to cell cycle inhibition. p53 activates p21 which deactivates [cyclin-dependent kinase 2](/wiki/Cyclin-dependent_kinase_2 "Cyclin-dependent kinase 2")(Cdk 2). Without Cdk 2, [retinoblastoma protein](/wiki/Retinoblastoma_protein "Retinoblastoma protein") (pRB) remains in its active, hypophosphorylated form and binds to the transcription factor [E2F1](/wiki/E2F1 "E2F1"), an important [cell cycle](/wiki/Cell_cycle "Cell cycle") regulator.[[34]](#cite_note-Ben-Porath_2005-34) This represses the transcriptional targets of E2F1, leading to cell cycle arrest after the [G1 phase](/wiki/G1_phase "G1 phase").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

p16Ink4a also activates pRB, but through inactivation of [cyclin-dependent kinase 4](/wiki/Cyclin-dependent_kinase_4 "Cyclin-dependent kinase 4") (Cdk 4) and [cyclin-dependent kinase 6](/wiki/Cyclin-dependent_kinase_6 "Cyclin-dependent kinase 6") (Cdk 6). p16Ink4a is responsible for the induction of premature, stress-induced senescence.[[34]](#cite_note-Ben-Porath_2005-34) This is not irreversible; silencing of p16Ink4a through promotor methylation or deletion of the p16Ink4a locus allows the cell to resume the cell cycle if senescence was initiated by p16Ink4a activation.

Senescence-associated secretory phenotype (SASP) [gene expression](/wiki/Gene_expression "Gene expression") is induced by several [transcription factors](/wiki/Transcription_factor "Transcription factor"), including [C/EBPβ](/wiki/CEBPB "CEBPB"), of which the most important is [NF-κB](/wiki/NF-%CE%BAB "NF-κB").[[35]](#cite_note-pmid27543988-35) Aberrant [oncogenes](/wiki/Oncogene "Oncogene"), DNA damage, and [oxidative stress](/wiki/Oxidative_stress "Oxidative stress") induce [mitogen-activated protein kinases](/wiki/Mitogen-activated_protein_kinase "Mitogen-activated protein kinase"), which are the upstream regulators of NF-κB.[[36]](#cite_note-pmid32300964-36)

Inhibition of the mechanistic target of rapamycin ([mTOR](/wiki/MTOR "MTOR")) suppresses cellular senescence, hence cellular senescence is inhibited by [rapamycin](/wiki/Sirolimus "Sirolimus").[[37]](#cite_note-pmid34878722-37)

## Characteristics of senescent cells

Senescent cells are highly heterogeneous, which has caused most authorities in the field to believe that a universal marker of senescent cells will not be found, and that a multi-marker approach is required for the detection of senescent cells.[[38]](#cite_note-pmid37400722-38) For this reason, the [Cellular Senescence Program Network](https://sennetconsortium.org/) was created to identify and characterize senescent cells in different body tissues.[[38]](#cite_note-pmid37400722-38)[[39]](#cite_note-pmid36936385-39)

Senescent cells are especially common in [skin](/wiki/Skin "Skin") and [adipose tissue](/wiki/Adipose_tissue "Adipose tissue").[[10]](#cite_note-pmid32752135-10) Senescent cells are usually larger than non-senescent cells.[[40]](#cite_note-pmid32686219-40) Transformation of a dividing cell into a non-dividing senescent cell is a slow process that can take up to six weeks.[[40]](#cite_note-pmid32686219-40)

Senescent cells affect tumor suppression, wound healing, and possibly embryonic/placental development, and play a pathological role in age-related diseases.[[20]](#cite_note-Physiological_and_pathological_cons-20) There are two primary tumor suppressor pathways known to mediate senescence: [p14arf](/wiki/P14arf "P14arf")/[p53](/wiki/P53 "P53") and [INK4A/RB](/wiki/INK4 "INK4").[[4]](#cite_note-Collado_2007-4) More specifically p16INK4a-pRb tumor suppressor and p53 are known effectors of senescence. Most cancer cells have a mutated p53 and p16INK4a-pRb, which allows the cancer cells to escape a senescent fate.[[41]](#cite_note-Kuilman_2008-41) The p16 protein is a cyclin-dependent kinase (CDK) inhibitor, and it activates the Rb tumor suppressor.[[42]](#cite_note-cell_removal-42) p16 binds to CDK 4/6 to inhibit the kinase activity and inhibit Rb tumor suppressor via phosphorylation.[[43]](#cite_note-Rayess_2012-43) The Rb tumor suppressor has been shown to associate with E2F1 (a protein necessary for transcription) in its monophosphorylated form, which inhibits transcription of downstream target genes involved in the G1/S transition.[[44]](#cite_note-44) As part of a feedback loop, increased phosphorylation of Rb increases p16 expression that inhibits Cdk4/6. Reduced Cdk4/6 kinase activity results in higher levels of the hypo-phosphorylated (monophosphorylated) form of Rb, which subsequently leads to reduced levels of p16 expression.[[43]](#cite_note-Rayess_2012-43)

The removal of aggregated p16 INK 4A-positive senescent cells can delay tissue dysfunction and ultimately extend life. In the 2011 *Nature* paper by Baker et al. a novel transgene, INK-ATTAC, was used to inducibly eliminate p16 INK4A-positive senescent cells by action of a small molecule-induced activation of [caspase 8](/wiki/Caspase_8 "Caspase 8"), resulting in apoptosis. A BubR1 H/H mouse model, known to experience the clinicopathological characteristics of aging-infertility, abnormal curvature of the spine, sarcopenia, cataracts, fat loss, dermal thinning, arrhythmias, etc., was used to test the consequences of p16INK4a removal. In these mice, p16 INK4a aggregates in aging tissues, including skeletal and eye muscle, and adipose tissues. Baker et al. found that if the senescent cells are removed, it is possible to delay age-associated disorders. Not only does p16 play an important role in aging, but it also in auto-immune diseases like rheumatoid arthritis that progressively lead to mobility impairment in advanced disease.[[42]](#cite_note-cell_removal-42)

In the nervous system, senescence has been described in astrocytes and microglia, but is less understood in neurons.[[22]](#cite_note-:4-22) Because senescence arrests cell division, studies of senescence in the brain were focused mainly on glial cells, and fewer studies were focused on nondividing neurons.[[45]](#cite_note-45) Analyzing single-nucleus RNA-Seq data from human brains suggested p19 as a marker for senescent neurons, which are strongly associated with neurons containing neurofibrillary tangles.[[46]](#cite_note-46)

### SASP

The [secretome](/wiki/Secretome "Secretome") of senescent [cells](/wiki/Cell_(biology) "Cell (biology)") is very complex. The products are mainly associated with [inflammation](/wiki/Inflammation "Inflammation"), [proliferation](/wiki/Cell_proliferation "Cell proliferation"), and changes in the [extracellular matrix](/wiki/Extracellular_matrix "Extracellular matrix").[[47]](#cite_note-pmid18555777-47)[[41]](#cite_note-Kuilman_2008-41) A [Senescence Associated Secretory Phenotype](/wiki/Senescence-associated_secretory_phenotype "Senescence-associated secretory phenotype") (SASP) consisting of inflammatory [cytokines](/wiki/Cytokine "Cytokine"), [growth factors](/wiki/Growth_factor "Growth factor"), and [proteases](/wiki/Protease "Protease") is another characteristic feature of senescent cells.[[48]](#cite_note-pmid272358512-48) There are many SASP effector mechanisms that utilize [autocrine](/wiki/Autocrine_signaling "Autocrine signaling") or [paracrine](/wiki/Paracrine_signaling "Paracrine signaling") signalling. SASP induces an [unfolded protein response](/wiki/Unfolded_protein_response "Unfolded protein response") in the [endoplasmic reticulum](/wiki/Endoplasmic_reticulum "Endoplasmic reticulum") because of an accumulation of unfolded proteins, resulting in [proteotoxic](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/proteotoxicity "wikt:proteotoxicity") impairment of cell function.[[49]](#cite_note-pmid31153901-49) [Autophagy](/wiki/Autophagy "Autophagy") is [upregulated](/wiki/Downregulation_and_upregulation "Downregulation and upregulation") to promote survival,[[49]](#cite_note-pmid31153901-49)[[50]](#cite_note-pmid29620049-50) while [inflammaging](/wiki/Inflammaging "Inflammaging") is simultaneously induced.[[51]](#cite_note-51)[[52]](#cite_note-52)

Considering cytokines, SASP molecules [IL-6](/wiki/Interleukin_6 "Interleukin 6") and [IL-8](/wiki/Interleukin_8 "Interleukin 8") are likely to cause senescence without affecting healthy neighbor cells. [IL-1beta](/wiki/Interleukin_1_beta "Interleukin 1 beta"), unlike IL-6 or IL-8, is able to induce senescence in normal cells with paracrine signaling. [IL-1beta](/wiki/Interleukin_1 "Interleukin 1") is also dependent on cleavage of IL-1 by [caspase-1](/wiki/Caspase_1 "Caspase 1"), causing a pro-inflammatory response.[[53]](#cite_note-pmid23770676-53) Growth factors, [GM-CSF](/wiki/GM-CSF "GM-CSF") and [VEGF](/wiki/VEGF "VEGF") also serve as SASP molecules.[[54]](#cite_note-54) From the cellular perspective, cooperation of transcriptional factors [NF-κB](/wiki/NF-%CE%BAB "NF-κB") and [C/EBP](/wiki/C/EBP "C/EBP")β increase the level of SASP expression.[[41]](#cite_note-Kuilman_2008-41)[[55]](#cite_note-55) Regulation of the SASP is managed through a transcription level autocrine [feedback loop](/wiki/Feedback_loop "Feedback loop"), but most importantly by a continuous [DDR](/wiki/DNA-damage_response "DNA-damage response").[[56]](#cite_note-56)[[57]](#cite_note-57) Proteins [p53](/wiki/P53 "P53"), [p21](/wiki/P21 "P21"), [p16ink](/wiki/P16 "P16")4a,[[58]](#cite_note-58) and [Bmi-1](/wiki/BMI1 "BMI1") have been termed as major senescence signalling factors, allowing them to serve as markers.[[59]](#cite_note-59) Other markers register morphology changes, reorganization of [chromatin](/wiki/Chromatin "Chromatin"), [apoptosis](/wiki/Apoptosis "Apoptosis") resistance, altered metabolism, enlarged cytoplasm or abnormal shape of the [nucleus](/wiki/Cell_nucleus "Cell nucleus").[[60]](#cite_note-Salama_2014-60) SASPs have distinct effects depending on the cellular context, including inflammatory or anti-inflammatory and tumor or anti-tumor effects. While considered a pro-tumorogenic effect, they likely support already tumor-primed cells instead of shifting healthy cells into transformation.[[60]](#cite_note-Salama_2014-60) Likewise, they operate as anti-tumor protectors[[61]](#cite_note-61) by facilitating the elimination of damaged cells by [phagocytes](/wiki/Phagocyte "Phagocyte"). The SASP is associated with many age-related diseases, including type 2 diabetes[[62]](#cite_note-62) and atherosclerosis.[[13]](#cite_note-pmid2664649922-13) This has motivated researchers to develop [senolytic](/wiki/Senolytic "Senolytic") drugs to kill and eliminate senescent cells to improve health in the elderly.[[13]](#cite_note-pmid2664649922-13) The nucleus of senescent cells is characterized by senescence-associated heterochromatin foci (SAHF) and DNA segments with chromatin alterations reinforcing senescence (DNA-SCARS).[[60]](#cite_note-Salama_2014-60)

## Clearance of senescent cells by the immune system

Due to the heterogeneous nature of senescent cells, different immune system cells eliminate different senescent cells.[[63]](#cite_note-pmid24114507-63)[[64]](#cite_note-pmid32164335-64) Specific components of the [senescence-associated secretory phenotype](/wiki/Senescence-associated_secretory_phenotype "Senescence-associated secretory phenotype") (SASP) factors secreted by senescent cells attract and activate different components of both the [innate](/wiki/Innate_immune_system "Innate immune system") and [adaptive immune system](/wiki/Adaptive_immune_system "Adaptive immune system").[[63]](#cite_note-pmid24114507-63)

[Natural killer cells](/wiki/Natural_killer_cell "Natural killer cell") (NK cells) and [macrophages](/wiki/Macrophage "Macrophage") play a major role in the clearance of senescent cells.[[65]](#cite_note-pmid30811627-65) Natural killer cells directly kill senescent cells, and produce [cytokines](/wiki/Cytokine "Cytokine") which activate macrophages which remove senescent cells.[[65]](#cite_note-pmid30811627-65) Senescent cells can be [phagocytized](/wiki/Phagocytosis "Phagocytosis") by [neutrophils](/wiki/Neutrophil "Neutrophil") as well as by macrophages.[[66]](#cite_note-pmid31088710-66) [Senolytic drugs](/wiki/Senolytic "Senolytic") which induce [apoptosis](/wiki/Apoptosis "Apoptosis") in senescent cells rely on [phagocytic](/wiki/Phagocytosis "Phagocytosis") immune system cells to remove the apoptosed cells.[[64]](#cite_note-pmid32164335-64)

Natural killer cells can use [NKG2D](/wiki/NKG2D "NKG2D") [killer activation receptors](/wiki/Killer_activation_receptor "Killer activation receptor") to detect the [MICA](/wiki/MHC_class_I_polypeptide%E2%80%93related_sequence_A "MHC class I polypeptide–related sequence A") and [ULBP2](/wiki/ULBP2 "ULBP2") [ligands](/wiki/Ligand_(biochemistry) "Ligand (biochemistry)") which become [upregulated](/wiki/Downregulation_and_upregulation "Downregulation and upregulation") on senescent cells.[[16]](#cite_note-pmid32298812-16)[[67]](#cite_note-Radosavljevic_2002-67) The senescent cells are killed using [perforin](/wiki/Perforin "Perforin") pore-forming [cytolytic](/wiki/Cytolysis "Cytolysis") protein.[[66]](#cite_note-pmid31088710-66) [CD8+ cytotoxic T-lymphocytes](/wiki/Cytotoxic_T_cell "Cytotoxic T cell") also use NKG2D receptors to detect senescent cells, and promote killing similar to NK cells.[[66]](#cite_note-pmid31088710-66)

Aging of the immune system ([immunosenescence](/wiki/Immunosenescence "Immunosenescence")) results in a diminished capacity of the immune system to remove senescent cells, thereby leading to an increase in senescent cells.[[65]](#cite_note-pmid30811627-65) Chronic inflammation due to SASP from senescent cells can also reduce the capacity of the immune system to remove senescent cells.[[66]](#cite_note-pmid31088710-66) [T cells](/wiki/T_cell "T cell"), [B cells](/wiki/B_cell "B cell"), and NK cells have all been reported to become senescent themselves.[[68]](#cite_note-pmid28687479-68) Senescent-like aging CD8+ cytotoxic T-lymphocytes become more [innate](/wiki/Innate_immune_system "Innate immune system") in structure and function, resembling NK cells.[[69]](#cite_note-pmid27867379-69) Immune system cells can be recruited by SASP to senescent cells, after which the SASP from the senescent cells can induce the immune system cells to become senescent.[[64]](#cite_note-pmid32164335-64)

[Chimeric antigen receptor T cells](/wiki/Chimeric_antigen_receptor_T_cell "Chimeric antigen receptor T cell") have been proposed as an alternative means to senolytic drugs for the elimination of senescent cells.[[64]](#cite_note-pmid32164335-64) [Urokinase receptors](/wiki/Urokinase_receptor "Urokinase receptor") have been found to be highly expressed on senescent cells, leading researchers to use [chimeric antigen receptor T cells](/wiki/Chimeric_antigen_receptor_T_cell "Chimeric antigen receptor T cell") to eliminate senescent cells in mice.[[70]](#cite_note-pmid32601490-70)[[71]](#cite_note-pmid32555459-71) Chimeric antigen receptor natural killer cells have been proposed as an [allogeneic](/wiki/Allotransplantation "Allotransplantation") means of eliminating senescent cells.[[72]](#cite_note-pmid32518575-72)

## Transient senescence

It is important to recognize that cellular senescence is not inherently a negative phenomenon. During [mammalian embryogenesis](/wiki/Mammalian_embryogenesis "Mammalian embryogenesis"), programmed cellular senescence plays a role in tissue remodeling via [macrophage](/wiki/Macrophage "Macrophage") infiltration and subsequent clearance of senescent cells.[[73]](#cite_note-Muñoz-Espín_2013-73) A study on the [mesonephros](/wiki/Mesonephros "Mesonephros") and [endolymphatic sac](/wiki/Endolymphatic_sac "Endolymphatic sac") in mice highlighted the importance of cellular senescence for eventual [morphogenesis](/wiki/Morphogenesis "Morphogenesis") of the embryonic kidney and the inner ear, respectively.[[73]](#cite_note-Muñoz-Espín_2013-73)

### Subjects

## Abstract

Cellular senescence, first described in vitro in 1961, has become a focus for biotech companies that target it to ameliorate a variety of human conditions. Eminently characterized by a permanent proliferation arrest, cellular senescence occurs in response to endogenous and exogenous stresses, including telomere dysfunction, oncogene activation and persistent DNA damage. Cellular senescence can also be a controlled programme occurring in diverse biological processes, including embryonic development. Senescent cell extrinsic activities, broadly related to the activation of a senescence-associated secretory phenotype, amplify the impact of cell-intrinsic proliferative arrest and contribute to impaired tissue regeneration, chronic age-associated diseases and organismal ageing. This Review discusses the mechanisms and modulators of cellular senescence establishment and induction of a senescence-associated secretory phenotype, and provides an overview of cellular senescence as an emerging opportunity to intervene through senolytic and senomorphic therapies in ageing and ageing-associated diseases.

## Abstract

Cellular senescence is a process that results from a variety of stresses and leads to a state of irreversible growth arrest. Senescent cells accumulate during aging and have been implicated in promoting a variety of age-related diseases. Cellular senescence may play an important role in tumor suppression, wound healing, and protection against tissue fibrosis; however, accumulating evidence that senescent cells may have harmful effects in vivo and may contribute to tissue remodeling, organismal aging, and many age-related diseases also exists. Cellular senescence can be induced by various intrinsic and extrinsic factors. The pathways for the proteins p53/p21 and p16Ink4a/retinoblastoma protein are important for irreversible growth arrest and senescent cells. Senescent cells secrete numerous biologically active factors; the specific secretion phenotype by senescent cell contributes to physiological and pathological consequences in organisms. The purpose of this article is to review the molecular basis of cell-cycle arrest and the senescent-associated secretory phenotype within these cells contributing to pathological consequences.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Cellular senescence is an irreversible growth arrest triggered by stressors, characterized by persistent DNA damage signaling, altered metabolism, and distinct morphological changes in cells.

enriched_points.point_title: Role in aging and age-related diseases
enriched_points.expanded_content: Senescent cells accumulate with age, contributing to tissue dysfunction and promoting diseases like frailty, diabetes, and neurodegeneration through chronic inflammation and impaired tissue repair.

enriched_points.point_title: SASP and its impact on tissue function
enriched_points.expanded_content: The senescence-associated secretory phenotype (SASP) involves inflammatory cytokines and growth factors that disrupt tissue homeostasis, promoting inflammation and influencing nearby cell behavior adversely.

Senescent cells exhibit increased resistance to apoptosis, allowing their persistence in tissues, which may exacerbate age-related pathologies and contribute to an environment conducive to tumorigenesis.
Senolytic therapies aim to selectively eliminate senescent cells, potentially improving healthspan and mitigating age-related diseases by reducing SASP effects and restoring tissue function.
